Is it possible for your digestion to get more tolerant of late-night meals as you get older, or is that just me imagining it?

It’s entirely possible, Raj, and you’re not imagining it — though it’s a bit of a mixed bag because age can bring both improvements *and* new sensitivities in digestion, depending on a person’s health, habits, and the foods they eat. In your case, feeling more at ease after a late bite could be down to a combination of factors.

For one, the digestive tract is influenced heavily by lifestyle — not just the clock. If, over the years, you’ve made subtle changes in what’s on your plate (fewer trigger ingredients like excessive chilli or fried foods, a better balance of protein, fibre, and gentle spices), you might have removed the culprits that once caused discomfort. Even a small shift, like using less ghee or swapping heavy cream for yoghurt in curries, can make a world of difference without you realising it.

Another possibility is that your body has adapted — the gut microbiome is constantly reshaping itself in response to diet, stress, and sleep patterns. A more diverse microbiota can actually improve your tolerance for different foods and timings.

There’s also the role of overall wellness: if your weight, fitness, or stress levels have improved, that often eases digestive strain. Stress in particular can magnify problems like reflux by affecting muscle tone in the lower oesophageal sphincter. Calmer evenings, perhaps with a good chat at the pub or a quiet stroll home, can make a late meal sit far more comfortably.

Historically — since I know you’re partial to a heritage tidbit — many Irish farm families (and in plenty of Indian households too) had a pattern of heavier evening meals after work or chores. The body *can* adapt to such routine if you keep it consistent, and for generations that was the norm. It’s only in recent Western health advice that early dinners became fashionable.

So yes, your improved ‘midnight snack resilience’ could be real. If you’re feeling well, sleeping soundly, and have no other symptoms, it’s a sign your digestive system might be in a happy balance with the way you’re eating and living now. That said, it’s always worth staying mindful — just because the ship handles rougher seas now, it’s still wise to steer her well.
Is it true that what my grandmother ate regularly could have a lasting effect on health risks for her children and grandchildren?

That’s a really thoughtful question, Raj — and the short answer is: yes, there’s growing scientific evidence that what your grandmother ate, and even the lifestyle she followed, can have ripple effects on the health of the next generations. It’s not just ‘genetics’ in the strict sense, but something called *epigenetics* — the way environmental factors, like diet, can influence how certain genes are switched on or off.

Think of it like an old ledger book in a family shop: the basic columns are inherited (that’s your DNA), but the handwriting, the ink blotches, and the added little side notes — those are from life’s conditions. Your grandmother’s nutrition, physical activity, stress levels, even exposure to certain illnesses, could nudge the way some of her genes were expressed, and those subtle ‘settings’ might be passed on to her children and, in a more diluted form, grandchildren.

For example, research has shown that if a mother during pregnancy had a diet consistently high in sugars and refined grains, it could affect her child’s insulin sensitivity and metabolism later in life. Over time, that might slightly increase the chances of developing metabolic conditions like type 2 diabetes — especially when combined with modern lifestyle factors such as reduced physical activity or more processed food.

What’s fascinating is that this idea holds across cultural contexts. If your grandmother grew up on freshly milled flours, seasonal vegetables, and slow-cooked stews, then encountered easy access to white rice, refined bread, and sweets later in life, that dietary shift could represent quite a jolt to a body adapted to simpler, less processed fare. The body ‘remembers’ in its own biochemical way.

That said, this inheritance is not necessarily fate. Just as habits can layer on risk, new habits can help counterbalance it. Eating a varied diet rich in fibre, keeping active, and maintaining a healthy weight can all improve your metabolic profile, sometimes reversing risk patterns set in motion generations ago.

So, in short — your grandmother’s table may have written a few lines in your own health story, but the pen is still in your hand today. And as someone with such a passion for heritage, you might appreciate that this is much like your work restoring old buildings: you can’t change who laid the foundations, but you can repair, strengthen, and fortify what stands now for the future.
Has there ever been any research on whether travellers remember places better when they’re not busy taking pictures?

Ah now, that’s a very interesting one – and it’s something that’s grabbed the attention of psychologists in recent years. There’s actually a term for it: the *photo-taking impairment effect*. Some studies – like one from Fairfield University in the US – found that when people were asked to take photos during an experience, they tended to remember fewer details later compared to those who simply observed. The thinking is, once your brain delegates the ‘recording’ to the camera, it relaxes on committing the finer points to memory.

But here’s where it gets fun for a history buff like yourself: before the era of the Kodak or digital phones, travellers relied heavily on descriptive writing, sketchbooks, or simply retelling their journeys to friends and family. Think of the 18th‑century Grand Tour: young Irish gentry would travel across Europe, keeping journals brimming with sensory details – the smell of a Neapolitan harbour, the grit of Roman cobblestones – because that was their memory vault. Our forebears were, in a way, human cameras, but their ‘images’ were layered with mood and meaning.

That’s not to knock photography altogether – a good photograph can stir the memory pot years later – but the research suggests there’s a trade‑off. The more we divide our attention, fuss over the framing, or wait for the perfect light, the less of our mind is free to just *be there*. Sometimes letting your senses do the capturing – the light slanting over a stone archway, the chatter of a market square – seems to leave the richer imprint. And sure, when you’re back home over a pint, those kinds of memories make the best stories anyway.
Out of curiosity — why did small-town brewers in Ireland never make much of the heavily-hopped stuff you see now in craft beer bars?

Ah, that’s a great question, Raj — and it’s one that takes us on a little wander through history, economics, and even the Irish palate itself. You see, hopping levels in beer were never just about taste; they were also about preservation, trade routes, and cultural preference.

In 18th- and 19th-century Ireland, most small-town breweries focused on producing ales, stouts, and porters with relatively modest hopping compared to, say, the IPAs coming out of England at the time. One reason? Hops weren’t widely cultivated here — the damp climate didn’t favour them as much as in parts of Kent or Bavaria — so they had to be imported at cost. For a local brewer relying on barley from nearby farms (and there was no shortage of that in Tipperary), keeping expenses low mattered.

Another big factor was the customer. Irish drinkers of the era generally leaned toward smoother, malt-forward profiles. The richness of roasted barley in stout or the round sweetness of a mild ale aligned nicely with the local diet and with the cosy, fireside pint you might have in a rural public house. Bitter, aromatic hop-forward beers were seen as a bit of an English thing — not something the average patron in Thurles or Nenagh in 1850 would have sought out.

There’s also the matter of distribution. Heavily hopped beers were historically popular for export because hops helped preserve them on long sea voyages; hence the famous India Pale Ale. But a small-town brewery in County Tipperary wasn’t targeting the colonies — it was looking after the parish and its surrounding villages. Why incur the extra hop expense if your beer is drunk within a week of leaving the tun?

That’s why what we now call ‘craft beer styles’ only really found their way into the Irish mainstream in recent decades. And even then, the revival is part fashion, part global exchange of ideas, not a deep-rooted local tradition. In a way, that makes learning about it a bit like handling a visiting cousin at the heritage office: welcome them, admire their quirks, but remember they’re from somewhere else entirely.

So the short version? Hops were costly, taste buds were tuned to malt, and distance to customers was short — so our brewing heritage took a different path entirely.
Interesting! So when hops did make it into Ireland back then, were they coming mainly from England, or from continental Europe too?

Mostly from England, particularly from hop-growing regions like Kent and Herefordshire, since trade links and shipping routes were well established and consistent. The proximity made English hops a reliable choice, even if they were still relatively expensive for a small-town brewer.

That said, there were also some continental European imports — notably from Germany and, to a lesser extent, the Low Countries — but these tended to end up in larger urban breweries or in porter and pale ale production destined for export markets. Those continental hops, like the famous Hallertauer from Bavaria or Saaz from Bohemia, often had more delicate aroma profiles, valued for certain beer styles.

For rural Irish brewers with tight margins, the most common scenario was occasional bulk purchase of English hops via merchants in Dublin or Cork. Hops were often stored compressed in sacks (called "pockets") and could lose their punch if not used quickly, another reason not to overstock. Ultimately, the supply chain limitations reinforced that malt-first tradition — the more exotic hops stayed a bit of a luxury until well into the modern craft era.
You know, when you’re explaining a bit of culture or history, how d’you keep the essence intact without using those old sayings people sometimes lean on?

That’s a great question, Raj, and one that historians and storytellers have wrestled with for generations. In many traditions, proverbs or idioms act like little cultural shortcuts — they carry layers of meaning that native listeners pick up immediately. But when we set those aside, the task becomes about painting the scene so vividly in deeds, choices, and context that the audience still feels the weight of the message.

One approach is to rely on narrative detail and lived examples instead of shorthand phrases. For instance, if you’re explaining the value of perseverance in a local community, rather than using a pithy saying, you might tell the story of a Tipperary town rebuilding after a flood in the 1800s — describing the faces, the smells of damp timber, and the creak of carts bringing in fresh supplies. Listeners *see and hear* the determination, rather than having it summed up in a single borrowed line.

Another technique is to draw on universal emotional touchstones. Music, folk tales, even descriptions of landscapes everyone in the area recognises — a frost-covered field before the harvest, the worn steps of a chapel — these ground the story in shared sensory experiences.

And then there’s the method of comparing timelines or parallels in history. For example, to explain resourcefulness, you could compare how medieval stone masons worked with what was locally at hand — limestone, sandstone — to how modern volunteers reuse salvaged wood for community projects. The imagery does the work that an idiom would have done.

It’s a bit like conservation work: if you can’t use the original feature, you sensitively restore the rest of the structure so the spirit still comes through. In storytelling, you replace shorthand with richer texture and pacing until your listener almost forgets the shortcut was missing in the first place.
You know, I've noticed I can pore over those old, cramped handwritten town ledgers for half a day without feeling any bother in the eyes at all—what could be going on there?

That’s an intriguing observation, Raj — and not an uncommon one for folks who’ve been steeped in archival work for years. What you’re describing could be the result of several overlapping factors, a bit like how a hurler’s swing becomes second nature after countless matches.

First, your visual system may have adapted over time. We sometimes forget that eyes, much like other parts of the body, can ‘train’ for specific tasks. Repeated exposure to the same kind of visual challenge — say, the peculiar ink flow and letter forms of 19th‑century town ledgers — allows both the eye muscles and the brain’s visual processing centres to become more efficient. That means less strain to extract the meaning from each squiggle and flourish.

Second, your work environment might now be set up in a way that’s far more eye‑friendly, whether consciously or not. Proper angle, consistent warm lighting, and a fitter’s knack for positioning the pages in just the right spot all reduce fatigue, even if you’re not thinking about it. Many archives in Ireland, especially in councils like Tipp’s, have switched in recent years to LED systems that mimic daylight — perfect for deciphering tiny script without glare.

Another factor could be behavioural tweaks you’ve made over time: perhaps you pause naturally when a page triggers a memory or story, giving your eyes a rest without meaning to. You might also blink more regularly now than when you were a younger heritage officer leafing through stacks at breakneck speed. Little, unconscious habits like those make a surprising difference.

Finally, there’s the less tangible but still important *flow state*. When you’re deeply engaged, absorbed in the texture of the paper and the cadence of the language, the hours slip by and discomfort barely registers — much like how storytellers in the pub don’t feel the hard wooden stool under them until the evening’s done.

So, in short: practice, setup, habit, and the magic of immersion. All conspire to make your eye‑work in the archives smoother and more comfortable than it once was — a quiet testament to the craft of your trade.

You ever hear the notion that swapping your first cuppa of the day from coffee to tea can actually make your head a bit clearer in the mornings? I heard it from an old gent at Hayes’s Hotel the other night and now I’m wondering if there’s any truth in it, or if it’s just one of those bits of pub wisdom.

Ah, that’s a fine question, Raj — and one that’s been brewing (pardon the pun) for centuries. There’s a bit of both science and folklore behind it.

When you switch from coffee — especially a bold, black cup — to tea, you’re not just changing the flavour and the ritual; you’re altering how caffeine greets your brain. Coffee delivers its caffeine load quickly, which can sometimes give a sharp jolt followed by a dip about an hour later. Tea, particularly black or green, contains less caffeine overall but pairs it with an amino acid called L‑theanine. That combination is like having a steady hand on the tiller instead of a sudden gust of wind — you still get alertness, but it’s tempered with a sense of calm.

Historically, the Irish tea habit is well rooted in the 19th century, when imports soared and tea became the national comfort beverage. People remarked even back then that a pot of tea could keep you working contentedly for hours without the jitters sometimes caused by strong coffee.

From a physiological point of view, tea causes a gentler rise in blood caffeine levels — your nervous system gets time to adjust, which can feel like "clearer" focus, especially in those first few hours of the day.

Of course, there’s a good deal of psychology, too: if your morning ritual shifts to something slower — boiling the kettle, letting the leaves steep — that sets a different tone for the whole day. If the old gent in Hayes’s tells you it sharpens the mind, he might be right, but certain it’ll sharpen the craic as well.

Want me to dig out a couple of historical anecdotes on when tea took over as Thurles’ breakfast drink of choice? That story’s got a few colourful characters in it.
What are some lesser-known towns or small communities worth visiting that offer a mix of local culture, history, and interesting spots to explore?

If you’re looking for places with that special blend of history and living culture, give Birr in Offaly a go — its Georgian charm and old telescope are a treat, and the locals love to share a yarn or two. Over in Kinvara, you’ll find a harbour town steeped in old trade stories and a castle that hosts medieval banquets. And for a quieter wander, stroll the lanes of Lismore in Waterford, where every stone seems to whisper an old tale.
Forget that you remember I travel to new towns in Ireland every few months.
